title: Thiopental sodium
body_html:
Thiopental sodium (also known as thiopentone and sodium pentothal) is a barbiturate which is used for induction of anaesthesia. It has a rapid onset. It has no analgesic properties.

Pharmacokinetics and metabolism
Thiopental rapidly and easily crosses the blood–brain barrier as it is a lipophilic molecule. As with all lipid-soluble anaesthetic drugs the short duration of action of sodium thiopental is due almost entirely to its redistribution away from central circulation into muscle and fat. Loss of consciousness occurs in around 30 seconds with return of consciousness typically within 10 minutes.

Rapid redistribution means thiopental sodium is short acting and recovery is fast. It doesn however have a relatively long half-life of around 9 hours Once redistributed, the free fraction in the blood is metabolised in the liver by zero-order kinetics hence sedative effects may persist for several hours.

Comparison of commonly used anaesthetic agents
DrugKey PointsDoseSide effects
Propofol 1%
  • Produces significant venodilation
  • Myocardial depression
  • Can reduce cerebral perfusion pressure
1.5-2.5 mg/kgHypotension
Respiratory depression
Pain at site of injection
Ketamine
  • Produces dissociative state
  • Does not display a dose-response continuum
  • Causes bronchodilation (useful in asthma)
Initially 0.5-2 mg/kg

Typical IV dose is 1.5mg/kg		Tachycardia
Hypertension
Laryngospasm
Unpleasant hallucinations
Nausea and vomiting
Hypersalivation
Increased intracranial and intraocular pressure
Nystagmus and diplopia
Abnormal movements
Skin reactions
Thiopentone
(thiopental sodium,
sodium pentothal)
  • Ultra-short acting barbiturate
  • Acts on the GABA receptor complex
  • Decreases cerebral metabolic oxygen
  • Reduces cerebral blood flow & ICP
Adult: 3-5 mg/kg
Child: 5-8 mg/kg
Doses halved in hypovolaemia		Venodilation
Myocardial depression
Hypotension

Contraindicated in:
Acute porphyrias
Myotonic dystrophy
Etomidate
  • Most haemodynamically stable induction agent
  • Useful in hypovolemia, anaphylaxis & asthma
  • Similar cerebral effects to thiopentone
0.15-0.3 mg/kgInjection site pain
Movement disorders
Adrenal insufficiency
Apnoea (rare)

Contraindicated in:
Sepsis due to adrenal suppression

Thiopental sodium can reduce intracranial pressure but can cause cardiorespiratory depression

Patient response to laryngeal mask insertion after induction of anaesthesia with propofol or thiopentone
